solar.2000:
In the year 2000, the solar story of Europe was just beginning, and the leaders were primarily clustered in Southern and Western Europe. Italy topped the list with 19 MW of installed solar capacity, followed by Switzerland (16 MW) and the Netherlands (13 MW). Spain showed early promise with a combined 19.3 MW split between on-grid (10.3 MW) and off-grid (9 MW) installations. France (7 MW) and Austria (5 MW) rounded out the Western bloc, reflecting early government support for renewable energy adoption in those regions.

Meanwhile, Northern and Eastern Europe remained in the infancy stages of solar deployment. Countries like Sweden (3 MW), Finland (2 MW), the UK (2 MW), and Denmark and Portugal (1 MW each) had minimal capacity. Czechia, representing Eastern Europe, barely registered with 0.1 MW. This early solar map highlighted not only the technological and economic gaps across the continent but also foreshadowed the regional solar revolutions that would take place over the next two decades.
solar.2010:

This early solar map highlighted not only the technological and economic gaps across the continent but also foreshadowed the regional solar revolutions that would take place over the next two decades.
solar.2012:
In 2012, solar energy in Europe saw a dramatic geographic shift, with several Eastern and smaller nations emerging as unexpected leaders in growth. Romania led the pack, skyrocketing its total capacity by 41 MW, marking a 4000% increase. Denmark followed closely, increasing its capacity to 402.3 MW, a 2323% surge. Meanwhile, Lithuania and Croatia, previously not major players in the solar conversation, made notable entries with 2233% and 1233% growth, respectively. Their progress reflected a growing continental commitment to solar power even among smaller economies.

The standout in absolute terms was Bulgaria, which added 768 MW, reaching a total of 922 MW a 498.7% increase that dwarfed the rest in scale. This wave of adoption from countries once on the solar periphery marked a turning point in the solar expansion of Europe.
solar.2014:
In 2014, Eastern Europe continued to surprise the continent with its rapid solar adoption—but this time, it was Russia making headlines. With a staggering 24236% growth, the country ramped up its total capacity to 306.4 MW. Right behind it, Poland nearly hit 1000% growth, bringing its capacity to 26 MW.

Moreover, Bosnia and Herzegovina joined the momentum, adding over 5 MW and increasing by more than 435%. Even Hungary and Estonia saw notable gains, with Hungary reaching 89 MW and Estonia crossing the 3 MW mark, both more than doubling their solar footprints.
solar.2016:
By 2016, the solar scene in Europe was no longer dominated by the usual countries. This year, the focus shifted to smaller nations, such as Kosovo, which increased its capacity by more than 1800%, reaching 1.95 MW, followed by Belarus, which was not far behind—adding 41 MW with a 683% growth.

Ireland, long known for its wind rather than sunshine, also jumped ahead, boosting its capacity to 12.19 MW, a 140% increase in just one year. Finland, despite its northern latitude, reached 37 MW, while Moldova doubled its solar footprint to 2 MW.
solar.2017:
Between 2017 and 2019, Poland, Estonia, and Hungary emerged as top performer in Europe, showing up year after year with impressive gains. Poland expanded its capacity by 93.4% in 2018 and nearly doubled again in 2019 with 178.9% growth, with a strong national push toward clean energy. Estonia mirrored this momentum with 112.7% growth in 2018, followed by an even steeper 278.1% increase in 2019, while Hungary maintained steady growth at 111.6% and 92.3%, respectively.

Alongside these consistent leaders, smaller nations like Kosovo, Latvia, Andorra, and Albania made higher growth often from a small base, but with big implications. Their rapid rise reflects a broader trend: solar adoption in Europe.
solar.2018:

Alongside these consistent leaders, smaller nations like Kosovo, Latvia, Andorra, and Albania made higher growth often from a small base, but with big implications. Their rapid rise reflects a broader trend: solar adoption in Europe.
solar.2020:
From 2020 to 2024, solar energy growth in Europe continued at a steady pace, but the spotlight shifted toward smaller nations making consistently strong gains. Republic of Moldova, North Macedonia, Lithuania, and Ireland emerged as the most consistent performers, appearing multiple times in the top five with notable growth. Republic of Moldova maintained high growth for four consecutive years, with increases like 236.6% in 2021, 315.3% in 2022, and 233.6% in 2023, showing a strong national commitment. North Macedonia led with an astronomical 255,461% growth in 2020 and remained in the top ranks through 2023 (251.4%) and 2024 (64.6%).

Lithuania also maintained momentum with growth rates of 88.1% (2020), 72.6% (2021), and 93.9% (2024), reflecting steady national-scale solar deployment. Ireland followed a similar path with 59.3% growth in 2020, 157.8% in 2023, and continued expansion. Smaller countries like Montenegro, Latvia, and Serbia showed standout years, with Montenegro hitting 502.8% in 2023 and Latvia at 1479.3% in 2022, while Serbia saw consistent growth in 2021 and 2022. These patterns suggest that while larger markets like Poland continue scaling in size, it is the smaller, agile nations that are increasingly shaping the solar growth narrative across Eastern and Central Europe.

solar.2000:
In the year 2000, not many countries in Africa have any capacity for Solar-powered energy. Take a look at the worldwide scaling and Africa looks completely plain. However, take a chance with regional scaling, and we can see that Morocco suddenly pops as the lead, with 6.7 MW as its max capacity.

It is followed by Tunisia (0.837 MW), Niger (0.5 MW), Malawi (0.218 MW), and Egypt (0.1 MW).
solar.2010: Jumping to 2010, worldwide scaling still doesn't do a service to the leaps that Africa takes. However, a local scale reveals that other countries steadily have joined the efforts towards solar energy. Morocco still leads with a growth of 149% with respect to the previous year, now being capable of 33.545 MW. The next closest is Algeria (25 MW), being followed by Egypt (15MW) and then by Senegal (7 MW). The difference is not that stark.
solar.2012: In 2012, Africa does not turn heads when looked at a global scale. At a local scale, Egypt has growth quite significantly. Sitting at a capacity of 35MW, it has reached Morocco, that seems to have a more stagnated growth at still 35.174 MW. Algeria has stagnated too, still at 25MW. South Africa has joined the efforts, now being highlighted with a total capacity of 11.713 MW.
solar.2014:
In 2014, South Africa has managed to barely make a dent at a global scale with a total capacity of 1163.79 MW. At a regional scale it's prevalence is impossible to ignore; the difference is so stark that every other country in the region seems as if they didn't have any capacity at all.

However, that's why we have our other visor that shows us Growth Percentage with respect to last year. This one is capped at 100%, so we can see that many countries are indeed making an effort towards expanding their capacity! All of these that have a maroon-like color are countries that have a growth beyond 100 percent, and while that may not be much in raw numbers, perhaps it is still an effort to be recognized. With this visor we can notice that, among those highlighted, the United Republic of Tanzania has grown to a total capacity of 11MW.
solar.2016:
At a worldwide scale, 2016 is not a year to write about for Africa. At a regional scale, South Africa still dominates with a total capacity of 2175.515 MW, having almost doubled with respect to two years ago. Also note that Morocco, previously at 118.8 MW, now sits at 201.87 MW. It has been overcome by Algeria, with a capacity of 261.74 MW.

Looking at the growth percentage with respect to last year (note that we stepped two years, but are looking at last year here), efforts are still ongoing on many, many countries. It is impressive to see South Africa, even while at the top, has grown by 60% with respect to last year.
solar.2017:
In the upcoming years, we will still see that at a worldwide perspective, Africa has been lost to the scale that other countries can reach. South Africa is simply not noticeable in the upcoming years when looking at it through the worldwide scaling, while at a regional scale it maintains its number one spot.

At a regional scale, we will see Egypt slowly raise its total capacity, expanding from 180 MW in 2017, to a whopping 2590 MW in 2024, it is an effort to recognize

If we look at the percentage growth from 2016 on, we will find ourselves in a complex situation; at this point it transforms into an exploration tool. The thing we can say with certainty is that countries all over the continent have a sort of ebb and flow in which some years they grow, some years they don't, but the work is definitely happening in Africa. In 2017 you will see a mostly orange map, indicating growth all around. Then in 2017, countries like Mauritania, Morocco, Chad, seem to put some hard work, only to disappear next year, leaving Angola, Zambia and Mozambique at the lead (percentage-wise only). You get the idea; countries' progress is not steady.

solar.2000:
In the year 2000, solar energy in Asia was still in its earliest phase, with countries like Nepal, Sri Lanka, Jordan, and Saudi Arabia just beginning to explore photovoltaic technology. Nepal had a humble 0.028 MW of off-grid solar, used primarily for rural electrification in remote regions. Sri Lanka showed slightly more momentum, with a combined total of 0.245 MW (split between 0.225 MW off-grid and 0.02 MW on-grid), reflecting early experimentation with both community-based systems and basic grid integration. Jordan, meanwhile, had a mere 0.015 MW, highlighting its minimal engagement with solar power at the time.

The outlier among them was Saudi Arabia, which, even in 2000, had the largest solar presence among the group with 0.35 MW of on-grid capacity. Still, these figures were modest by any standard and represented a time when solar energy was far from mainstream.
solar.2010:
In the year 2000, solar energy in Asia was still in its earliest phase, with countries like Nepal, Sri Lanka, Jordan, and Saudi Arabia just beginning to explore photovoltaic technology. Nepal had a humble 0.028 MW of off-grid solar, used primarily for rural electrification in remote regions. Sri Lanka showed slightly more momentum, with a combined total of 0.245 MW (split between 0.225 MW off-grid and 0.02 MW on-grid), reflecting early experimentation with both community-based systems and basic grid integration. Jordan, meanwhile, had a mere 0.015 MW, highlighting its minimal engagement with solar power at the time.

The outlier among them was Saudi Arabia, which, even in 2000, had the largest solar presence among the group with 0.35 MW of on-grid capacity. Still, these figures were modest by any standard and represented a time when solar energy was far from mainstream.
solar.2012:
In 2012, Asia's solar energy growth story highlighted a striking contrast between emerging contributors and large-scale developers. Afghanistan led in percentage growth, posting a staggering 13,335% increase, though it came from a minimal baseline, reaching just 16.1 MW in total. Similarly, Saudi Arabia grew by 375.4%, and Pakistan by 145.2%, signalling a strong regional push from countries that had previously lagged behind in solar adoption. These sharp percentage spikes underscored a new wave of enthusiasm, especially among Middle Eastern and South Asian countries, eager to transition to renewables amid rising energy demands.

On the other hand, when viewed by absolute growth, the picture shifted. India clearly dominated, adding a massive 416.3 MW, bringing its total capacity close to 1 GW a reflection of its early large-scale policy and infrastructure investments. Israel followed with 90.3 MW added, while Pakistan, Afghanistan, and Saudi Arabia maintained strong but smaller additions.
solar.2014:
In 2014, Kuwait made a dramatic entrance into Asia's solar energy scene, recording a staggering 3013% growth—albeit from a tiny baseline of just over 3.5 MW. Similarly, Lebanon (155.9%), Azerbaijan (140%), India (135.6%), and Yemen (130.2%) showed strong percentage growth, highlighting a wave of momentum from Middle Eastern and South Asian countries. These high rates, while numerically small for some nations, reflected an accelerating regional shift towards clean energy, particularly in countries with previously little-to-no solar presence.

Yet, in terms of absolute growth, the spotlight remained firmly on India, which added a massive 2173 MW, pushing its total capacity past 3.7 GW. Israel followed with 210 MW, and Pakistan, Cyprus, and Sri Lanka added 64.7 MW, 29 MW, and 9.85 MW, respectively. India's dual appearance in both rankings signaled its pivotal role in leading solar deployment across Asia. Meanwhile, countries like Kuwait and Lebanon, though small in output, underscored a rapidly diversifying solar landscape where both scale and startup momentum were helping shape Asia's energy transition.
solar.2016:
In 2016, West Asia and South Asia saw a surge in solar momentum, with countries like Jordan, Azerbaijan, and Kuwait emerging as standout performers in percentage growth. Jordan led the way with a remarkable 448% increase, closely followed by Azerbaijan (418.8%) and Kuwait (370.2%), each significantly expanding from smaller baselines. Even Pakistan and Lebanon recorded strong double- and triple-digit growth, at 122.1% and 118.4%, respectively. These numbers highlighted the region's growing urgency to diversify energy portfolios and address rising electricity demands through solar.

In terms of absolute growth, however, India remained unmatched, adding an impressive 4285 MW in a single year nearly 70% of the total capacity increase among the top five countries. Pakistan followed with a strong 324 MW, showing its emergence as a serious regional player. Jordan added 254 MW, aligning high percentage growth with meaningful scale. Meanwhile, Israel (110 MW) and Sri Lanka (35 MW) maintained steady contributions. This contrast between massive scale in larger economies and rapid relative gains in smaller ones illustrated a dynamic solar transformation across Asia where some countries accelerating from the ground up, others building on early leads.
solar.2017:
From 2017 to 2019, Asia's solar expansion was led by a mix of emerging contributors and long-standing powerhouses. India dominated in absolute terms across all three years, adding 8274 MW in 2017, 9227 MW in 2018, and 7765 MW in 2019, reflecting the country's consistent policy focus and investment in large-scale solar infrastructure. Israel and Jordan followed with steady contributions Israel added 100 MW, 340 MW, and 792 MW, while Jordan expanded by 106 MW, 342 MW, and 211 MW during the same period. Sri Lanka, though smaller in scale, showed a strong increase from 83 MW in 2017 to 121 MW by 2019, becoming a key regional example of solar transition among developing nations.

On the percentage side, however, the spotlight was shared with smaller and newer entrants. In 2017, Nepal (300.6%) and Oman (271%) posted some of the region's highest growth rates, while Armenia (621.7%), Oman (214%), and Yemen (145.8%) stood out in 2018. By 2019, Georgia (196.7%), Kuwait (147.9%), and Afghanistan (130.6%) were leading in percentage growth, despite adding only a few dozen megawatts. These rapid gains—though small in capacity reflect an accelerating momentum in countries. Together, the data paints a dynamic picture: India continued to lead in volume, but a growing number of Asian nations began catching up in speed.

solar.2020:
Between 2020 and 2024, India remained the undisputed leader in absolute solar capacity expansion, adding over 61,000 MW in just five years. With yearly increases like 4455 MW in 2020, 10,244 MW in 2021, and 24,524 MW in 2024, India continued to scale aggressively, cementing its position as the region's solar powerhouse. Other significant contributors included Israel, which added over 3,000 MW during the period, and Saudi Arabia, which posted a notable 2145 MW increase in 2023 and 1755 MW in 2024. Uzbekistan, Qatar, and Sri Lanka also showed strong yearly additions, steadily building national capacity while joining the broader regional transition to solar.

But when viewed through the lens of percentage growth, the momentum shifts to a different group of countries. Qatar and Uzbekistan stood out in 2022, growing by 15,686% and 5714% respectively—remarkable jumps from previously small baselines. Azerbaijan (450%), Oman (392%), and Lebanon (360%) also delivered exponential growth, highlighting how even late adopters were rapidly scaling up their solar infrastructure.

solar.2000:
In 2000, the solar journey of the Pacific was just beginning, and Australia clearly led the region with 25 MW of installed solar capacity far surpassing any other country. This dominant early investment in on-grid solar marked Australia as the only nation in Oceania with large-scale photovoltaic infrastructure at the time. The rest of the region, by contrast, featured fragmented off-grid systems intended for basic energy access. French Polynesia (0.548 MW) and New Caledonia (0.19 MW) followed distantly, with other Melanesian and Micronesian nations like Fiji, Micronesia (0.1 MW), and Kiribati contributing small yet symbolically significant capacities.

Many island nations including Tonga (0.057 MW), Cook Islands (0.047 MW off-grid and 0.01 MW on-grid), and Tuvalu and Palau, with just a few kilowatts each, were in the early experimentation phase. These micro-scale systems were largely off-grid and decentralized, reflecting their focus on rural electrification and energy resilience. While their contributions were minimal in absolute numbers, they laid the groundwork for the rapid solar adoption that would follow in the next two decades.

solar.2012:
In 2012, several Pacific Island nations made bold strides in solar energy, especially when measured by percentage growth. Samoa led with a staggering 2142.9% increase, followed by Tonga (543.7%), Fiji (226%), and Guam (133.3%). These jumps, though modest in scale, were significant signs of progress for countries with previously little to no solar infrastructure. Even Australia, already a leader in the region, saw a 76.7% increase, reinforcing its ongoing investment in renewable energy. These high growth rates revealed a deeper shift: many of these island nations were beginning to prioritize clean energy as a path to resilience and sustainability.

Looking at absolute growth, the story cantered more around scale. Australia clearly led the region, adding 1090 MW an expansion unmatched by its neighbours. Countries like French Polynesia (3.18 MW) and New Zealand (1 MW) contributed more modest additions, while American Samoa recorded a 2.416 MW gain from a possible new baseline. Interestingly, Tonga was the only nation to appear in both the top percentage and absolute growth rankings,
solar.2014:
In 2014, Samoa and Niue led the solar growth story of the Pacific region in terms of percentage increase, with Samoa expanding its solar capacity by 904% and Niue by 520%. These were remarkable gains from small baselines island nations. New Zealand also made an appearance on both lists, growing by 144% and adding 13 MW. Other small nations like Cook Islands (135%) and Micronesia (104%) also posted impressive growth, emphasizing how even microstates were beginning to contribute meaningfully to the energy transition of the region.

However, when it came to absolute growth, Australia once again dominated, adding a massive 825 MW to reach over 4.1 GW in total capacity—far outpacing any other country in the region. While its percentage increase (24.9%) was modest, the scale of deployment reaffirmed Australia's leadership in solar infrastructure. French Polynesia also made the list with 6.4 MW added, while Samoa and the Cook Islands remained notable for achieving high growth both in percentage and absolute terms.
solar.2016:
In 2016, Micronesia stood out as a rare example of a Pacific nation making waves in both absolute and percentage growth. It increased its solar capacity by 2.9 MW, translating to an impressive 328.8% growth, signaling a major shift in national energy priorities. In terms of percentage growth, it was joined by several small island nations making rapid progress: Vanuatu grew by 317.7%, Nauru by 238.1%, and Palau and the Solomon Islands by 118.4% and 91.6%, respectively.

On the absolute scale, however, the picture was led once again by Australia, which added 845 MW—bringing its total to 5.88 GW—a testament to its infrastructure strength despite a modest 16.8% growth rate. New Zealand also made a notable contribution with 16 MW added (43.2%), while Samoa and New Caledonia made 6.08MW and 3.3MW
solar.2017:
From 2017 to 2019, New Caledonia consistently emerged as a high-growth performer, appearing among the top countries in both percentage and absolute solar growth each year. It grew by 170.7% in 2017, 121.8% in 2018, and 72.1% in 2019, while adding over 17 MW, 33 MW, and 44 MW respectively. Meanwhile, New Zealand maintained a steady pace across all three years with absolute additions of 18 MW, 20 MW, and 24 MW, showing a gradual but firm commitment to solar. Australia, as expected, led the region in sheer scale adding 1362 MW in 2017, 3886 MW in 2018, and a further 4325 MW in 2019 though its percentage growth remained modest, averaging below 55% each year.

On the percentage front, smaller Pacific nations made some of the most remarkable strides. In 2019, Nauru posted a massive 200.9% growth, while Niue followed with 175.9%, demonstrating how even nations with small absolute capacity are accelerating their solar transitions. Vanuatu also maintained a strong presence, achieving over 74% growth in 2018 and continuing its upward trend from 2017.

solar.2020:
From 2020 to 2024, Australia and New Zealand dominated the Pacific's solar growth story in terms of absolute capacity, with Australia adding over 22,000 MW across five years and New Zealand steadily increasing from 145 MW in 2020 to 570 MW in 2024, adding over 400 MW. New Caledonia also made consistent appearances with sizable growth early in the period, particularly in 2021 (50 MW) and 2022 (35 MW), before slowing by 2023. While these nations led in total capacity, their percentage growth was relatively modest Australia's yearly growth ranged from 15% to 28%, indicating a mature market expanding steadily.

In contrast, smaller island nations led the narrative in percentage growth, signalling their rapid energy transformation. Palau stood out in 2023 with a dramatic 352% growth, while Nauru (2024) and Tonga (2022) saw increases of 92.8% and 104%, respectively. Even the Marshall Islands, Solomon Islands, and Papua New Guinea reported consistent year-over-year growth, reflecting growing access and investment.
